attributes.stem: A 28-year-old Aboriginal female is admitted to hospital with a two-month history of joint pain and fatigue. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 150/95 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. She has oral ulcers, non-scarring alopecia and synovitis in the small joints in both hands. Investigations reveal multiple abnormalities including positive antinuclear antibodies with a titre of 1:640 and sub-nephrotic range proteinuria. Following confirmation of the diagnosis on renal biopsy, she receives intravenous methylprednisolone followed by a high-dose oral prednisolone tail, hydroxychloroquine, and mycophenolate sodium.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following monitoring investigations is most important whilst this patient is taking hydroxychloroquine?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has been diagnosed with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) with lupus nephritis as a manifestation on renal biopsy, and appropriately commenced on hydroxychloroquine as part of their management regimen. Hydroxychloroquine is an antimalarial medication commonly used in SLE that can cause irreversible retinal toxicity, making regular ophthalmological screening essential.

Optical coherence tomography is currently considered the most sensitive test for early detection of hydroxychloroquine-induced retinopathy. It can detect subtle changes in the retinal layers before clinical symptoms develop. Guidelines recommend baseline screening within the first year of treatment and then annual screening after five years of use, or sooner in high-risk patients.

Full blood examination monitoring is important for patients on immunosuppressive medications like mycophenolate, but it is not the most critical test for hydroxychloroquine specifically. While hydroxychloroquine can rarely cause blood dyscrasias, this is not its main toxicity concern.

Thyroid function tests are not routinely required for hydroxychloroquine monitoring. Transthoracic echocardiogram is important in SLE patients to monitor for complications like pericarditis or valve disease, but it is not specifically required for hydroxychloroquine monitoring. Liver function tests are routinely monitored in patients on multiple medications, particularly with mycophenolate, but hepatotoxicity is not a major concern with hydroxychloroquine.

Regarding hydroxychloroquine-induced retinopathy, early detection through regular screening is crucial as retinal damage can progress even after cessation of the medication.

With regards to the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander population, there exists an increased incidence of SLE compared to non-Indigenous people. There also exists an increased rate of lupus nephritis as a manifestation of SLE, as well as increased rates of progression to end-stage kidney disease in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people with lupus nephritis.
learningPoints: Hydroxychloroquine requires regular ophthalmological screening due to risk of retinopathy.
Optical coherence tomography is the most sensitive test for early detection of hydroxychloroquine-induced retinopathy.
There are higher rates of SLE and lupus nephritis in the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander population.
references: Stokkermans TJ, Goyal A, Bansal P, Trichonas G. Chloroquine And Hydroxychloroquine Toxicity. In: StatPearls [online]. 2020. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537086/

Eades LE et al. Systemic lupus erythematosus in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples in Australia: addressing disparities and barriers to optimising patient care. The Lancet Rheumatology. Volume 6, Issue 10, e713 - e726. Available at: 10.1016/S2665-9913(24)00095-X

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
